title: Agentic Loops
tagline: Building with AI coding tools: MCP, skills, multi-agent workflows, memory, and your own agent from scratch.
audience: Professional AI researchers and senior engineers. Assumes fluency in coding and working knowledge of LLMs. Theory-heavy but practically grounded.
spine: Claude Code is the primary subject. Codex and opencode appear as contrast points that illuminate design tradeoffs, not as equal-weight coverage.

prose_rules: Avoid AI-sounding phrasing and hedging filler.
Prose teaches the concept; figures explain the model; the widget lets the reader manipulate it.
Written for experts. Do not over-explain fundamentals the audience already owns.

chapter_grammar.prose: Teaches the concept in expert-level prose.
chapter_grammar.figures: One or more SVG figures that explain the mental model, not decoration.
chapter_grammar.widget: One signature interactive React widget per chapter that lets the reader manipulate the core idea.
chapter_grammar.artifact: Every chapter ships a runnable artifact: real, executable code the reader can run, beyond inline snippets. This is a hard requirement for every chapter.
chapter_grammar.exercises: ExerciseCards at the end of each chapter.

source_material.research_corpus: The complete research corpus lives in docs/research/: one doc per chapter (chNN-slug.md), a cross-cutting survey (00-survey-overview.md), and a MANIFEST.md index. It is current as of 2026 and covers MCP spec versions, transports, the security surface, Claude Code internals, competitive frameworks, the multi-agent failure taxonomy, memory architectures, prompt caching, the three DIY-agent build stages, and evaluation. Before authoring a chapter, open its matching doc and treat it as the factual backbone. Verify only genuinely version-sensitive claims against current sources where the runner has web access.

parts.title: Extending the Agent: MCP and Skills

chapters.title: The Loop
chapters.summary: The agentic primitive: perceive, decide, act, observe. Why 'agent' names the loop, not the model. The minimal viable loop as the mental anchor for the whole book.

artifact.spec: A ~100-line agent loop over the Messages API that logs each phase (perceive, decide, act, observe) as it iterates. The reader can run it and watch the loop turn.

chapters.widget: Loop-state visualizer that steps through the four phases with the live message list at each step.

chapters.title: Anatomy of a Tool Call
chapters.summary: From token to execution and back. How function calls are represented to and emitted by the model, parsed, dispatched, and how results re-enter context. Messages API tool-use mechanics concretely.

artifact.spec: A tool-call tracer that runs a real tool-use exchange and prints the raw representation of each tool_use and tool_result block, showing exactly what the model sees.

chapters.widget: Interactive tool-call inspector: toggle between the rendered view and the raw block/token view of a call and its result.

chapters.title: Context-Window Economics
chapters.summary: The master constraint that explains most downstream design decisions. Token accounting, the cost curve, what fills a window and how fast. Sets up both Part IV (memory) and the multi-agent cost arguments.

artifact.spec: A context-budget analyzer that takes a running session and decomposes the window by category (system, tools, history, results) with token counts and cost.

chapters.widget: Context-budget visualizer: a live stacked breakdown of a window that the reader can perturb to see how fast categories grow.

chapters.title: The Landscape
chapters.summary: Claude Code, Codex, and opencode as points in a design space. What each optimizes and where the philosophies diverge. Deliberately not a feature list. Orientation for the rest of the book.

artifact.spec: A comparison harness that runs the same small task under different tool postures and surfaces the difference in loop behavior, not just output.

chapters.widget: Design-space map: an interactive positioning of the tools across axes (autonomy, context strategy, extensibility) with rationale on selection.

chapters.title: MCP from the Wire Up
chapters.summary: The protocol and its JSON-RPC underpinnings, the initialization handshake and capability negotiation, spec version history through the recent revisions.

artifact.spec: A minimal MCP server and client that complete the handshake and exchange framed messages the reader can inspect on the wire.

chapters.widget: Handshake sequence diagram that animates the initialize/capabilities exchange message by message.

chapters.title: Transports
chapters.summary: stdio versus SSE versus Streamable HTTP. Message framing, the tradeoffs, when each is correct.

artifact.spec: The same MCP server exposed over two transports with a switchable client, so the reader can watch identical calls travel differently.

chapters.widget: Transport comparison: side-by-side framing view of one call over stdio vs HTTP.

chapters.title: Resources, Tools, and Prompts
chapters.summary: The three MCP primitives, their exact semantics, and when to reach for each.

artifact.spec: One server exposing all three primitives with a client walkthrough exercising each in turn.

chapters.widget: Primitive explorer: pick a primitive and see its request/response shape and intended use.

chapters.title: Building a Real MCP Server
chapters.summary: Beyond the toy: lifecycle, error handling, official SDK patterns, a server that does something genuine.

artifact.spec: A production-shaped MCP server backed by a real integration (not echo), with proper lifecycle and error handling, runnable locally.

chapters.widget: Live server console: invoke the server's tools and inspect structured results and errors.

chapters.title: The MCP Security Surface
chapters.summary: Prompt injection through tool results, the confused-deputy problem, token-theft and command-injection vulnerability classes from recent security research, OAuth and auth patterns, and mitigations. The chapter most treatments skip.

artifact.spec: A deliberately vulnerable MCP server paired with its hardened counterpart, so the reader can run the exploit and then the mitigation.

chapters.widget: Attack/defense toggle: flip a server between vulnerable and hardened and watch a malicious tool result get neutralized.

chapters.title: Skills
chapters.summary: What a SKILL.md actually is, the frontmatter, progressive disclosure, and the discovery and triggering mechanics.

artifact.spec: A real, well-authored skill with correct frontmatter and progressive disclosure that the reader can drop in and trigger.

chapters.widget: Skill anatomy viewer: hover the parts of a SKILL.md to see how each drives discovery and loading.

chapters.title: Skill or Server
chapters.summary: The design decision: when a skill beats an MCP server and vice versa, and the hybrid. Authoring skills well.

artifact.spec: A skill-plus-server hybrid that solves one problem both ways, with the tradeoff made explicit in code and runnable both paths.

chapters.widget: Decision matrix: an interactive chooser that maps problem characteristics to skill, server, or hybrid.

chapters.title: Delegation
chapters.summary: Subagents and the Task tool, how context isolation works and why it is the whole point, and the orchestrator-worker pattern as the foundational structure.

artifact.spec: A working orchestrator that dispatches an isolated subagent and returns the result, with the context boundary observable.

chapters.widget: Isolation viewer: watch the parent and subagent contexts side by side and see what does and does not cross the boundary.

chapters.title: Coordination Patterns
chapters.summary: Supervisor and hierarchical architectures, agent-to-agent handoff, parallel fan-out and aggregation. LangGraph, CrewAI, and AutoGen surveyed as contrast for how each frames orchestration.

artifact.spec: A parallel subagent fan-out with result aggregation, runnable, showing concurrency and the join.

chapters.widget: Orchestration graph: an interactive supervisor/worker graph the reader can reconfigure to see routing change.

chapters.title: When Multi-Agent Fails
chapters.summary: The rigorous core of the part. Coordination cost, the empirical question of when multi-agent helps versus adding latency and expense, and the MAST failure taxonomy as a structured way to reason about breakdowns.

artifact.spec: An instrumented multi-agent run that exposes a real coordination failure, with the diagnosis and the fix runnable side by side.

chapters.widget: Failure taxonomy explorer: map an observed breakdown onto MAST categories and see the corresponding mitigation.

chapters.title: The Memory Taxonomy
chapters.summary: Working versus short-term versus long-term, episodic versus semantic. Mapping the concepts precisely before implementing, picking up the context-window thread from Chapter 3.

artifact.spec: A session harness that runs the same interaction under different memory regimes so the reader can compare behavior directly.

chapters.widget: Memory-regime switcher: toggle regimes and watch what the agent retains or forgets across turns.

chapters.title: Prompt Caching and the Economics of Remembering
chapters.summary: How prompt caching works, cache breakpoints, TTL, and how it reshapes agent design once accounted for. The load-bearing practical memory chapter.

artifact.spec: A cached-versus-uncached benchmark over the same workload that reports the cost and latency delta with real numbers.

chapters.widget: Cache-economics calculator: adjust breakpoints and hit rate and see the cost curve move.

chapters.title: Retrieval as Memory
chapters.summary: Vector stores, embeddings, RAG for agent memory, the actual database options and patterns, and where retrieval helps versus where it quietly fails.

artifact.spec: A retrieval-backed memory layer over a real vector store that the agent queries during a run, fully runnable.

chapters.widget: Retrieval inspector: issue a query and see what gets retrieved, ranked, and injected into context.

chapters.title: Self-Managed Memory
chapters.summary: The memory-tool pattern where the agent maintains persistent notes across runs, context editing and compaction, and summarization as the window fills. Letta/MemGPT paging, Mem0, and Zep surveyed as architectures.

artifact.spec: An agent that writes and reads its own persistent memory across sessions, demonstrating recall on a second run.

chapters.widget: Memory-file timeline: watch the agent's persistent notes get written, compacted, and reloaded across sessions.

chapters.title: Stage One: The Thin Wrapper
chapters.summary: A CLI over the API with a REPL. The smallest thing that deserves to be called a coding agent, on macOS.

artifact.spec: The stage-one agent: a runnable macOS CLI REPL that talks to the API and maintains a conversation.

chapters.widget: REPL walkthrough: an annotated transcript the reader can step through to see the wrapper's control flow.

chapters.title: Stage Two: The Real Loop
chapters.summary: Tool dispatch, planning, error handling and retries, file and shell tools. The wrapper becomes an agent.

artifact.spec: The stage-two agent with a working tool suite (file and shell), planning, and retry handling, runnable on macOS.

chapters.widget: Tool-dispatch tracer: watch the agent select, call, and recover from tools across a task.

chapters.title: Stage Three: Production-Grade
chapters.summary: MCP client support so the agent consumes the Part II servers, subagents so it delegates, plus streaming, permissions, and sandboxing. The synthesis chapter that pulls the book together.

artifact.spec: The stage-three agent: MCP-capable and subagent-capable, with streaming, a permissions model, and sandboxing, runnable on macOS.

chapters.widget: Capability panel: toggle MCP, subagents, and sandboxing and see the agent's behavior and safety envelope change.

chapters.title: Evaluating Agents
chapters.summary: The capstone. SWE-bench and SWE-bench Verified with their caveats, eval harness design, task construction, the agent failure taxonomy, and how to measure what this book built: skills, MCP servers, multi-agent systems, and memory.

artifact.spec: A small but real eval harness that scores an agent on a task set and reports results, runnable end to end.

chapters.widget: Eval dashboard: run the harness and inspect per-task pass/fail with failure-mode tags.
